Metadata.title: 1xSecret — Share one-time secrets, end-to-end encrypted
Metadata.description: Share passwords, API keys and other secrets as encrypted, self-destructing one-time links. Encrypted in your browser — the server never sees the plaintext. Open source & self-hostable.

Footer.tagline: Secrets are encrypted in your browser. The server only ever stores ciphertext.

SafeguardNotice.safeCreator: This instance runs in safeguarded mode. You are creating this secret from an allowed network, so anyone with the link (and password, if set) can retrieve it.
SafeguardNotice.unsafeCreator: This instance runs in safeguarded mode. You are creating this secret from outside the operator's allowed networks — it can only be retrieved from within those networks.

CreateForm.secretPlaceholder: Paste the secret you want to share — a password, an API key, a client secret …
CreateForm.charactersLeft: {count, plural, one {# character left} other {# characters left}}
CreateForm.passwordPlaceholder: Enter a password or generate one
CreateForm.passwordHint: Share the password through a different channel than the link — e.g. link by email, password by phone or chat. It is never sent to the server.
CreateForm.expiryAfter: after that, the secret is deleted.
CreateForm.errorGeneric: The secret could not be created. Please try again.
CreateForm.errorRateLimited: Too many requests — please wait a moment and try again.

CreateResult.heading: Your one-time link is ready
CreateResult.copyFailed: Copying failed — please select and copy the value manually.
CreateResult.passwordReminder: The recipient needs the password to open the secret. Share it through a different channel than the link, and don't lose it — it cannot be recovered.
CreateResult.restrictedNote: This secret was created from outside the allowed networks, so it can only be retrieved from within them (e.g. from the operator's office network or VPN).
CreateResult.onceNote: The link works exactly once. After the first view, the secret is destroyed.

RevealPage.title: You received a one-time secret
RevealPage.introAvailable: Someone shared a secret with you. It can be revealed exactly once — after that it is permanently destroyed. Only continue when you are ready to save it.
RevealPage.passwordPlaceholder: Enter the password you received separately
RevealPage.passwordRequired: This secret is protected with a password. The sender shared it with you through a separate channel.
RevealPage.onceWarning: This works exactly once — make sure you are ready to copy the secret now.
RevealPage.destroyedNote: This secret has now been destroyed on the server. The link no longer works — store the secret somewhere safe before closing this page.
RevealPage.unavailableTitle: This secret is no longer available
RevealPage.unavailableText: It has either been retrieved already, expired, or the link is invalid. If you never managed to view it, let the sender know — the secret may have been intercepted, or it simply expired.
RevealPage.restrictedTitle: Retrieval not possible from this network
RevealPage.restrictedText: This instance runs in safeguarded mode: the secret can only be retrieved from the operator's allowed networks (e.g. office network or VPN). Connect to such a network and try again — the secret has NOT been consumed by this attempt.
RevealPage.invalidPassword: Wrong password. The secret has not been consumed — you can try again.
RevealPage.invalidPasswordLocked: Wrong password. The secret is safe, but to slow down guessing, further attempts from your network are paused for {seconds, plural, one {# second} other {# seconds}}.
RevealPage.lockedOut: Too many attempts from your network. The secret is safe — please wait about {seconds, plural, one {# second} other {# seconds}} and try again.
RevealPage.invalidLink: This link is damaged: the key part after “#” is missing or invalid. Ask the sender to copy the complete link.
RevealPage.decryptFailed: Decryption failed. The link may be corrupted — ask the sender for a new secret.
RevealPage.errorGeneric: Something went wrong. Please try again.
RevealPage.errorRateLimited: Too many attempts — please wait a moment and try again.
RevealPage.replyHint: Want to answer with a sensitive value of your own?

MySecrets.title: My secrets
MySecrets.intro: References to secrets you created in this browser. Labels never leave this device — the server only knows anonymous ciphertext.
MySecrets.empty: You haven't created any secrets in this browser yet.
MySecrets.removeNote: Removes only the local reference, not the secret itself.
MySecrets.localOnly: This list is stored in your browser's local storage.

HomePage.heroTitle: Share a one-time secret — encrypted, self-destructing
HomePage.heroSubtitle: Send passwords, API keys and client secrets as links that work exactly once. Encrypted in your browser before anything is uploaded — the server never sees the plaintext, and nothing stays behind in inboxes.
HomePage.minimalSubtitle: Encrypted in your browser; viewable exactly once.
HomePage.how1Text: Paste the secret, optionally set a retrieval password, choose how long the link stays valid. Your browser encrypts everything locally and uploads only ciphertext.
HomePage.how2Text: Send the one-time link to the recipient — and the password, if you set one, through a different channel. The decryption key lives in the link fragment and is never sent to the server.
HomePage.how3Text: The recipient reveals the secret with one explicit click. The ciphertext is destroyed at that exact moment — the link is now worthless, and you can see when it was retrieved.
HomePage.whyText1: Anything you paste into an email or chat is archived forever — searchable, forwardable, and one mailbox breach away from being public. A one-time link removes the secret from that permanent record: after the first view there is nothing left to steal.
HomePage.whyText2: One-time semantics also make interception visible. If an attacker opens the link before the intended recipient, the recipient finds a dead link, you see the early retrieval — and you know to rotate the secret immediately. With a plain email, you would never find out.
HomePage.securityText1: 1xSecret encrypts with AES-256-GCM before upload. The key is derived from a random 256-bit master key that only exists in the link fragment (the part after “#”) — browsers never send fragments to servers, so the operator stores ciphertext it cannot decrypt.
HomePage.securityText2: An optional retrieval password is stretched with Argon2id and folded into the key derivation on your device. It is required for decryption itself, not just checked by the server — and it never leaves your browser.
HomePage.securityText3: Retrieval uses a cryptographic challenge: your browser proves possession of the key material with an Ed25519 signature before the server releases the ciphertext and destroys it. Link scanners and preview bots can never burn a secret, and a wrong password never consumes it.
HomePage.useCase1Text: Hand new colleagues or customers their first login without leaving it in a mailbox forever.
HomePage.useCase2Text: Deliver client secrets to integration partners so they can be viewed once, copied into a vault, and are gone.
HomePage.useCase3Text: WireGuard preshared keys, API tokens, signing keys, recovery codes — anything that must not live in chat history.
HomePage.useCase4Text: If it shouldn't be findable in “Sent” three years from now, it belongs in a one-time link.
HomePage.selfHostText1: 1xSecret is free software (AGPL-3.0). Run it with Docker Compose or the Helm chart on your own domain, bring your own PostgreSQL, and mount your own legal pages. German and English are built in.
HomePage.selfHostText2: The safeguarded mode restricts retrieval to your company networks: partners can send secrets to you and receive secrets from you, but third parties cannot use your instance to exchange secrets among themselves.
HomePage.faq1Q: What exactly happens when a secret is viewed?
HomePage.faq1A: The moment the recipient clicks “Reveal once”, the server verifies the cryptographic challenge, hands over the ciphertext and deletes it in the same atomic step. Only the retrieval timestamp is kept (30 days by default) so the creator can see that — and when — the secret was picked up.
HomePage.faq2Q: What if the link is intercepted?
HomePage.faq2A: If someone opens the link before your recipient, the recipient finds a dead link and tells you — interception becomes visible, and you rotate the secret. If you additionally set a retrieval password (shared over a different channel), the thief cannot open the secret at all: ten wrong attempts destroy it.
HomePage.faq3Q: What does the server actually see?
HomePage.faq3A: Ciphertext, a random salt, a public key for the challenge signature, and timestamps. Never the plaintext, never the key (it lives in the link fragment, which browsers don't transmit), never the password. Even a full database dump does not allow decryption.
HomePage.faq4Q: Why should I set a retrieval password?
HomePage.faq4A: The link alone is a single factor: whoever has it, has the secret. A password shared over a second channel (phone, chat, in person) turns it into two factors. In 1xSecret the password is part of the encryption key itself — it is never sent to or checked by the server.
HomePage.faq5Q: Can a mail scanner or link preview “use up” my secret?
HomePage.faq5A: No. Opening the page never consumes the secret. Retrieval requires an explicit click plus a signed cryptographic challenge that only a browser holding the key material (and password) can produce.
HomePage.faq6Q: How long is a secret stored?
HomePage.faq6A: You choose: 10 minutes to 1 month. Whatever is not retrieved in time is destroyed. After retrieval (or expiry), only an anonymous read-receipt row remains for 30 days, then it is deleted too.
HomePage.faq7Q: Can I see whether my secret was retrieved?
HomePage.faq7A: Yes. Your browser keeps a private, local list of the secrets you created (“My secrets”). It shows whether and when each one was retrieved — the label you give an entry never leaves your device.
HomePage.faq8A: A self-hosting option for companies. Secrets created from your allowed networks (CIDR ranges) are retrievable by anyone with the link; secrets created by outsiders can only be retrieved from inside those networks. Your instance stays useful for exchanging secrets with partners without becoming a free public service.
HomePage.faq9A: Yes — free and open source under the AGPL-3.0 license. You can use the hosted instance or run your own with Docker or Helm within minutes.
HomePage.faq10Q: How is this better than sending a password by email?
HomePage.faq10A: Email is stored forever, in at least two mailboxes, often unencrypted, and is searchable and forwardable. A one-time link keeps the secret out of the archive, makes interception detectable, expires automatically — and with a retrieval password even a compromised mailbox is not enough.

NotFound.title: Page not found
NotFound.text: This page does not exist — or the secret behind it is long gone.
